stories.title: Colliers Arranges Sale of 302K-SF Industrial Building in Kentucky
stories.excerpt: Colliers completed the sale of Gray Industrial Center Building 2, a newly completed 302,400-square-foot industrial facility at 530 Logistics Drive in Shelbyville, Kentucky, approximately 30 miles east of Louisville. The build-to-suit distribution facility is fully leased to Kuehne+Nagel, a global logistics provider. Colliers Industrial Capital Markets advisors Alex Cantu, Alex Davenport, Jeff Devine, Steve Disse and Tyler Ziebel, together with local CBRE leasing advisors Doug Butcher and Alex Grove, represented Gray Development. The property was sold to ElmTree Funds in a forward-funded acquisition that closed upon completion of the facility. “Gray Industrial Center Building 2 demonstrates the continued demand for build-to-suit industrial facilities in strategically located markets like Shelbyville, where access to the I-64 corridor and the greater Louisville region continues to attract both occupiers and institutional capital,” said Alex Cantu. “Forward-funded acquisitions of newly completed, fully leased assets remain highly attractive to investors seeking modern industrial product supported by long-term tenancy and strong market fundamentals.” Situated on approximately 32.19 acres, the Gray Industrial Center Building is located approximately one mile from Interstate 64. The property provides convenient access to Louisville International Airport and UPS Worldport. The post Colliers Arranges Sale of 302K-SF Industrial Building in Kentucky appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Kuehne+Nagel build-to-suit, 302K SF, Shelbyville KY (30mi E Louisville)
ElmTree Funds forward-funded acquisition upon completion; I-64 corridor access
Institutional capital continues backing fully-leased industrial; Louisville distribution hub strength
stories.summary: Colliers closed sale of Gray Industrial Center Building 2, a newly completed 302,400-SF build-to-suit distribution facility in Shelbyville, Kentucky, fully leased to logistics provider Kuehne+Nagel. ElmTree Funds acquired the asset via forward-funded structure upon delivery. The transaction underscores persistent institutional appetite for modern, fully-leased industrial product in strategic markets with I-64 access and proximity to Louisville's distribution ecosystem.
stories.impact: Louisville metro's industrial strength (proximity to UPS Worldport, airport, I-64) continues attracting institutional capital and corporate tenants, signaling sustained logistics demand. Multifamily developers should monitor ancillary labor inflows from logistics expansion; wage growth in warehouse/fulfillment roles may support rental demand in Louisville and Shelbyville submarkets, particularly for workforce/affordable units near employment centers.

stories.title: Marcus & Millichap Brokers Sale of Net-Leased Storming Crab Restaurant in Rockford
stories.excerpt: Marcus & Millichap announced the sale of a net-leased restaurant property occupied by Storming Crab in Rockford, Illinois.“This offering generated significant investor interest due to the property’s strong operating performance, prime location and the limited supply of comparable retail assets in the market,” said Brian Parmacek, managing director of investments. “The asset received multiple offers above the asking price, ultimately attracting a private investor focused on securing long-term cash flow and income generation.”Parmacek, investment specialist in Marcus & Millichap’s Chicago Downtown office, had the exclusive listing to market the property on behalf of the seller, RCJD Canyon Crest, LLC, and procured the buyer, MBOGO Properties, LLC. The 5,860-square-foot freestanding property is located at 6779 E. State St. and is occupied by Storming Crab, a chain of casual restaurants specializing in Cajun-style seafood boils. The restaurant is surrounded by national retailers, including Target, ALDI, Ulta Beauty, Hobby Lobby, Burlington and PetSmart and is less than a half-mile from Hard Rock Casino Rockford. The post Marcus & Millichap Brokers Sale of Net-Leased Storming Crab Restaurant in Rockford appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Net-leased Storming Crab, 5,860 SF, Rockford IL; multiple offers above asking
Hard Rock Casino Rockford proximity; Target, ALDI, Ulta, Hobby Lobby anchor tenants
Investor demand for limited retail supply; long-term cash flow focus
stories.summary: Marcus & Millichap brokered the sale of a net-leased 5,860-SF Storming Crab restaurant in Rockford, Illinois, to a private investor seeking income. The property received multiple offers above asking price, attributed to strong operating performance, prime location, and scarcity of comparable retail assets. Site benefits from proximity to Hard Rock Casino Rockford and national retail anchors.
stories.impact: Limited retail supply and strong investor appetite in Rockford suggests stabilizing consumer activity and real estate market confidence in the metro. For multifamily developers, retail scarcity and casino-driven foot traffic (Hard Rock opened 2024) signal renewed population engagement; monitor job growth and leisure spending in Rockford to gauge mid-market rental demand acceleration.

stories.title: Free-Market NoHo Apartments Generate Strong Buyer Demand
stories.excerpt: Marcus & Millichap finalized the sale of 312 Bowery, a multifamily property in Manhattan’s NoHo neighborhood, for $16.65 million. John Stewart and Dylan Torey of Marcus & Millichap exclusively marketed the property on behalf of the seller, Bremen House, Inc., and procured the buyer, Allied Realty & Development Corp. “Buyer demand was some of the strongest we’ve seen this year due to the scarcity of truly free-market multifamily properties,” said Stewart. “Well-maintained properties in core locations, with no historical rent regulation or with attorney-approved deregulation documentation, will continue to attract the most engaged buyers, with bidding wars and, in some cases, sub-five-cap final pricing.” The five-story, 23,000-square-foot building in the NoHo East historic district was built in 1920 and converted to multifamily in 2004. It includes 22 modernized free-market apartments. The property has approximately 7,000 square feet of unused development rights and a 3,900-square-foot ground-floor commercial space occupied by The Hole, an art gallery. The post Free-Market NoHo Apartments Generate Strong Buyer Demand appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: 312 Bowery, Manhattan NoHo; 22 free-market units, $16.65M sale
Sub-five cap rates; buyer competition over deregulation/free-market status
7,000 SF unused development rights; 1920 historic, 2004 multifamily conversion
stories.summary: Marcus & Millichap closed the $16.65M sale of 312 Bowery, a 23,000-SF historic NoHo building with 22 modernized free-market apartments. Buyer demand was notably strong, with bidding wars and sub-five-cap pricing driven by scarcity of truly free-market multifamily assets in Manhattan. The property retains 7,000 SF of unused development rights and ground-floor retail occupied by The Hole art gallery.
stories.impact: Manhattan free-market multifamily commands premium valuations and attracts aggressive bidding—a signal of capital chasing deregulated units amid limited supply. For TCR, this underscores the regulatory arbitrage value in stabilized, deregulated NYC assets and highlights that development-rights upside (7K SF untapped here) commands buyer attention even on smaller, historic properties. Rent regulation scarcity is driving cap compression and competition in core Manhattan.

stories.title: Nicholas & Associates Tapped as Contractor for Ground-Up Multifamily Development in Madison
stories.excerpt: Nicholas & Associates, the construction management and general contracting arm of the Nicholas Family of Companies, has been selected by Moyer Properties as the general contractor for Aster Flats, a 263-unit, three-building luxury rental community set on a 4.86-acre site in Madison, Wisconsin. The ground-up development is the latest in Nicholas & Associates’ Wisconsin portfolio, further expanding the firm’s presence in the state and region. Designed by HKM Architects + Planners, Inc., Aster Flats will offer studio, one-, two- and three-bedroom units ranging in size from 588 to 1,395 square feet. “Being selected to build Aster Flats reflects the trust and strong reputation we’ve established in southeast Wisconsin through a series of high-profile projects in recent years,” said Nick Papnicholas Jr., chief executive officer and managing director of Nicholas Family of Companies. Nicholas & Associates will break ground in August on the new development, with construction happening in phases across the three buildings. Completion is anticipated in February 2028. If you’re reading about the multifamily industry, you should be in the room with its leaders. Connect Apartments brings together the executives featured in Connect CRE’s coverage for an afternoon of high-level insights and unmatched networking on September 22 at The Biltmore in LA. The post Nicholas & Associates Tapped as Contractor for Ground-Up Multifamily Development in Madison appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Aster Flats, Madison WI: 263 units, 3 buildings, luxury; Nicholas & Associates GC
Ground-up delivery phased; August 2025 groundbreak, February 2028 completion
Studio to 3-bed, 588–1,395 SF; southeast Wisconsin market momentum
stories.summary: Nicholas & Associates was selected as general contractor for Aster Flats, a 263-unit, three-building luxury ground-up development on 4.86 acres in Madison, Wisconsin, developed by Moyer Properties. Construction is phased, breaking ground August 2025 with completion targeted for February 2028. Units range studio to three-bedroom, 588–1,395 SF.
stories.impact: Madison is adding significant luxury multifamily supply (263 units phased over 2.5 years); developers should track absorption trends and rent growth post-delivery to gauge market saturation. Nicholas & Associates' repeated project selection in southeast Wisconsin suggests local contractor capacity and developer confidence in the region's growth fundamentals (UW-Madison, tech recruitment, young professional base). Monitor competing projects' delivery timeline to assess competitive positioning on rents and lease-up pace.

stories.title: Largely Vacant Santa Monica Offices Get New Life as School Campus
stories.excerpt: Colliers arranged the all-cash sale of Ocean Park Plaza, a two-story, 100,700-square-foot office and medical building at 2701 Ocean Park Blvd. in Santa Monica. The buyer, an undisclosed co-educational school, plans to relocate to the property and establish its new campus there. Vice chair Sean Fulp, EVPs Mark Schuessler and Todd Tydlaska and associate VPs Jordan Garcia and Blake Hammerstein of Colliers marketed the property and facilitated the transaction on behalf of the seller. Tim Dornin and Scott Rigsby of Industry Partners and Dan Pickart of Newmark represented the buyer. The sale repositions a, largely vacant office building for active community use. Ocean Park Plaza had approximately 73% of its space available for immediate occupancy. The acquisition returns the asset to full utility while removing it from the area’s office inventory. “This is a great outcome for Santa Monica,” said Fulp. “The buyer gets a campus in the heart of the Westside, and the neighborhood gains an institution that adds real activity and stability.” The post Largely Vacant Santa Monica Offices Get New Life as School Campus appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Ocean Park Plaza, Santa Monica: 100.7K SF office, 73% vacant; school buyer all-cash
Two-story office repositioned for educational use; removes supply from office inventory
Westside supply relief; office-to-alternative-use conversion trend
stories.summary: Colliers facilitated the all-cash sale of Ocean Park Plaza, a 100,700-SF Santa Monica office/medical building that was 73% vacant, to an undisclosed co-educational school planning to relocate its campus to the site. The transaction removes underutilized office space from market inventory and converts the asset to active community use.
stories.impact: Santa Monica's weak office market (73% vacancy at Ocean Park) is driving asset redeployment away from office—a bellwether of office structural decline on the Westside. For multifamily developers, this signals reduced office-driven employment demand in Santa Monica; however, educational institutions attracting families and staff may create ancillary rental demand for workforce housing near the new campus, particularly in nearby submarkets. Monitor whether other large Santa Monica office buildings face similar conversion pressure.

stories.title: Apartment Complex Sells for $51M in Los Angeles’ Miracle Mile
stories.excerpt: Prime Residential acquired the 132-unit complex in the Miracle Mile neighborhood for $51.3 million. An LLC tied to Studio City-based Harrison Properties sold the property at 740 South Burnside Avenue, named Palm Court Apartments, for approximately $388,000 per unit. Marcus & Millichap’s Jeff Louks, Gayle Factor and Elliot Sabag represented the seller, while Matt Ziegler procured the buyer. Ziegler said the seller recently completed extensive upgrades to the property. Palm Court is near Museum Row on Wilshire Boulevard, as well as the Grove shopping complex, the Original Farmers Market, and Metro’s new station at Wilshire and Fairfax Avenue. It was completed in 1988 with 48 one-bedroom apartments, 72 two-bedrooms and 12 three-bedrooms. It also features a pool and spa, a fitness center, saunas and a rooftop sundeck. The acquisition comes as multifamily investment activity picks up in L.A. Investment sales increased roughly 25 percent during the 12-month period ending in March, with L.A. accounting for more than 20 percent of all apartment trades across primary U.S. markets, according to Marcus & Millichap’s most recent market report. However, the city’s Measure ULA transfer tax continues to weigh on larger deals, as transactions above $10 million during the same period were down 50 percent compared to the year before the measure took effect in 2023. L.A.’s multifamily vacancy rate held at 4.8 percent at the start of 2026, while average effective rents increased 1.4 percent since the same period in 2025 to $2,853 per month. Marcus & Millichap forecasts vacancy will reach 5 percent by the end of this year as 8,500 units are added to the market. Prime Residential has owned the landmark Park La Brea community just north of Miracle Mile since 1995. It’s said to be the largest apartment complex west of the Mississippi River. It includes 4,249 rent-controlled units in 18 high-rise towers and 175 garden-style buildings on 144 acres. Gregory Cornfield can be reache
bullets: Palm Court Apartments, LA Miracle Mile: 132 units, $51.3M ($388K/unit)
Prime Residential buyer; Museum Row, Grove, Metro Wilshire-Fairfax station nearby
12-month investment sales up 25%; LA 20% of all primary US apartment trades
stories.summary: Prime Residential acquired Palm Court Apartments, a 132-unit complex in Los Angeles' Miracle Mile neighborhood, for $51.3 million ($388K per unit). Built in 1988, the property features 48 one-bedroom, 72 two-bedroom, and 12 three-bedroom units plus amenities. The acquisition reflects a 25% increase in LA multifamily investment over 12 months, with LA accounting for over 20% of primary US apartment sales.
stories.impact: LA multifamily trades surging 25% YoY and representing 20% of national activity signal strong investor appetite for the metro despite rent pressures and supply pipeline. Miracle Mile's proximity to Metro Wilshire-Fairfax, retail anchors, and cultural institutions supports stable demand; transit-oriented value-add plays are attracting capital. For TCR, this reaffirms LA's outsized share of national institutional investment—monitor competing deliveries and rent growth in Miracle Mile and adjacent Central LA submarkets; supply-demand balance is tightening post-transit infrastructure completion.

stories.title: Columbia University Acquires Student Housing in Morningside Heights for $122M
stories.excerpt: Columbia University is adding a new student housing project to its dormitory portfolio in northern Manhattan. Sam Charney’s Charney Companies sold its more than 300-unit project at 99 Claremont Avenue in Morningside Heights to the Ivy League university for $122 million, a source close to the deal confirmed. The Real Deal first reported the deal. Charney, along with Criterion Real Estate Capital, acquired the 80,000-square-foot building, which was originally a smaller 82-unit dorm next to Riverside Church, from an affiliate of the church for $38 million in 2024, with plans to convert it into luxury student housing. The joint venture secured $55 million of acquisition financing from Madison Realty Capital to purchase and redevelop the historic property, then known as McGiffert Hall, as Commercial Observer previously reported. “The opportunity to restore a historic, irreplaceable 92-year-old building with all the beauty that 99 Claremont offers while providing critically needed best-in-class student housing to New York City is a tremendous one,” Sam Charney said in 2024 following the acquisition. The finished project will be Columbia’s first residence hall dedicated to students in its School of General Studies (GS), according to a press release from Columbia in March. The dormitory will open in the fall. “This milestone reflects our commitment to meeting one of the most significant needs within our student population: accessible, community-centered housing designed specifically for nontraditional undergraduate students,” Lisa Rosen-Metsch, dean of Columbia’s School of General Studies, wrote in the March release. “Its close proximity to Morningside campus will reduce commute times and increase opportunities for students to deepen their engagement with one another, essential for the community building that is a hallmark of the GS experience.” Spokespeople for Charney and Columbia did not immediately respond to requests for comment. In addition to housing more than 300 st
bullets: Columbia University acquires 99 Claremont Ave., Morningside Heights: 300+ units, $122M
Charney Companies & Criterion bought McGiffert Hall for $38M (2024); $55M acquisition financing
University expansion into student housing; rapid flip to institutional buyer
stories.summary: Columbia University acquired 99 Claremont Avenue in Morningside Heights for $122 million, a 300+-unit student housing project originally purchased by Charney Companies and Criterion Real Estate Capital for $38 million in 2024. The joint venture financed the redevelopment with $55 million in acquisition debt from Madison Realty Capital before selling to the university. The historic 92-year-old building was originally an 82-unit dormitory (McGiffert Hall).
stories.impact: Columbia's $122M acquisition signals institutional demand for student housing in Manhattan and validates conversion economics (38M buy → 122M sale in ~18 months). However, the developer's exit to the university rather than hold the stabilized asset suggests higher institutional bid-up of student housing or builder hesitation on permanent placement in tight Manhattan financing environment. For TCR, this indicates student housing remains institutional-investor-targeted in NYC; ground-up development economics for private student housing may face headwinds if universities are aggressively acquiring and consolidating dorm supply. Monitor other Ivy/large universities' acquisition activity as potential demand suppression for private builds.

stories.title: Miami design board swoons over OKO Lilli, 13th Floor’s Nobu condo towers
stories.excerpt: Vlad Doronin’s second condo tower in Edgewater and 13th Floor Investments’ proposal for a Nobu-branded residential building next to a historic Brickell church are advancing.  The Miami Urban Development Review Board voted in favor of both developments on Wednesday, with most board members gushing over the designs of what would be the latest luxury tower additions to the city’s quickly evolving skyline. One board member voted no on Doronin’s planned building, citing issues with some of the design.  Miami-based OKO Group, led by Doronin, plans the 53-story, 117-unit project, called OKO Lilli Tower, on the half-acre lot at 717 Northeast […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: OKO Lilli (Doronin), Miami Edgewater: 53 stories, 117 units, luxury condo; UDRB approved
13th Floor Nobu 619 Brickell: 75 stories, 321 units; Brickell Ave, adjacent historic church
Luxury condo towers advancing; Miami design board strong support for prestige brands
stories.summary: Miami's Urban Development Review Board approved two luxury residential projects: OKO Lilli, a 53-story, 117-unit tower led by Vlad Doronin in Edgewater fronting Biscayne Bay with 350-foot public baywalk, and 619 Brickell by Nobu, a 75-story, 321-unit tower by 13th Floor Investments on Brickell Avenue adjacent to First Miami Presbyterian Church. Both projects feature high design and are advancing toward city planning/zoning approval.
stories.impact: Miami's approval momentum for two large luxury condo projects (438 units combined, prestige branding) signals strong developer confidence in condo absorption and pricing power. However, condo-focused supply does not directly compete with rental apartments. For TCR's multifamily lens: Monitor if luxury condo presales (Miami River deals above $3.7K/SF) cannibalize high-end rental demand, or if condo construction labor/materials cost escalation (both are competing for resources) raises multifamily development costs. Brickell and Edgewater remain investor-favored corridors; track rental comps in adjacent multifamily as luxury condo competition may suppress rents at mid-market rentals.

stories.title: CRE Comes Together at MADE Bush Terminal for Cocktails and an Exciting New Development
stories.excerpt: On a humid and rainy July night in Sunset Park, Brooklyn, people from throughout the commercial real estate industry escaped the elements at the new mixed-use complex MADE Bush Terminal, which hosted Commercial Observer’s annual summer networking mixer. Amid a series of massive square wooden columns conveying the complex’s industrial feel, attendees networked, enjoyed drinks and small bites, and heard from some of MADE’s pioneering tenants on the benefits of taking space at the complex, which, in addition to sites like a brewery and a new 1,000-capacity music venue, will mostly host companies with light industrial uses. Bridget Chansakul, assistant vice president at the New York City Economic Development Corporation (NYCEDC), handles leasing for the development, and was excited for people to see the goings-on at Building A, where the event took place. While several tenants are already in place in that building, as well as in the project’s Building B, the full development and unveiling of MADE Bush Terminal, which will eventually be a five-building complex, will occur slowly over the next few years. “We’re giving a sneak peek tonight, but it’s just the beginning of what’s happening here in Sunset Park,” said Chansakul. “We’re getting ready to announce a new brewery coming to the site and we’re going to be unveiling a new building, so it’s just the beginning of an exciting and much larger project.” After brief introductions from Brian Pascus, a finance reporter at Commercial Observer, and Jennifer Brown, senior vice president for the Sunset Park district at NYCEDC, attendees were treated to a brief discussion for a deeper introduction to MADE Bush Terminal. Waverly Neer, vice president of asset management at NYCEDC, held a short panel discussion with two of the building’s earliest tenants: lighting manufacturer Stickbulb and design studio Pelle. Pelle, run by husband and wife architect team Jean and Oliver Pelle, moved into Building
bullets: MADE Bush Terminal, Sunset Park Brooklyn: light industrial, brewery, 1K-capacity venue
Mixed-use adaptive reuse; industrial, retail, entertainment tenants leasing
Brooklyn waterfront transformation; NYCEDC-supported development
stories.summary: MADE Bush Terminal, a new mixed-use complex in Sunset Park, Brooklyn, hosted Commercial Observer's summer networking event. The project repurposes the industrial Seaholm Power Plant site into light industrial, brewery, music venue (1,000 capacity), and other commercial tenants. NYCEDC's Bridget Chansakul handles leasing; Building A is substantially leased.
stories.impact: MADE Bush Terminal exemplifies Brooklyn's industrial-to-mixed-use conversion trend and signals strong occupier demand for light industrial, creative office, and entertainment uses in Sunset Park. However, as a mixed-use/light-industrial project, it does not directly add multifamily supply. For TCR: Monitor if such mixed-use developments in Sunset Park/Red Hook drive neighborhood amenity improvements and foot traffic, which could support multifamily rental demand. Proximity to waterfront reactivation and cultural venues may justify transit-oriented rental projects; track residential leasing velocity in nearby multifamily (Industry City, Domino Sugar sites) to gauge whether industrial-use neighborhoods are attracting residential investment.

stories.title: Palm Beach County nixes proposed Project Tango data center
stories.excerpt: Plans for a proposed data center in Palm Beach County hit a major roadblock Wednesday after county commissioners rejected a zoning application that would have allowed the project’s development.  At the end of a 12-hour hearing, officials voted 5-1 to deny an amended zoning application that would have permitted for a 1 million-square-foot data center at 20125 State Road 80/Southern Boulevard, the South Florida Business Journal reported. The project also would have included 2.3 million square feet of warehouses and 216,000 square feet for minor utility use.  Plans for Central Park Commerce Center, also known as Project Tango, were filed […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Palm Beach County denies Project Tango: 1M SF data center + 2.3M SF warehouses + 216K utility
PBA Holdings (joint venture) zoning application rejected 5-1; 200-acre site
Community opposition to infrastructure impact; resubmission possible without prejudice
stories.summary: Palm Beach County commissioners voted 5-1 to deny zoning for Project Tango, a proposed 1 million-SF data center and 2.3 million-SF warehouse complex on ~200 acres in unincorporated county land. PBA Holdings (joint venture: Palm Beach Aggregates, Phillips Inc., Klein, Ogden Cap) owns ~130 acres. Community opposition centered on infrastructure and environmental impacts. The denial was made without prejudice, allowing resubmission.
stories.impact: Project Tango's rejection removes a major speculative industrial project from South Florida's pipeline, reducing supply competition for conventional logistics/warehouse rentals in Palm Beach County. However, community resistance to large-scale data center/industrial development signals tightening zoning and entitlement environment in exurban South Florida. For TCR: If data centers face local pushback, traditional industrial and warehouse supply may tighten, supporting fundamentals for multifamily in adjacent labor markets. However, blocked mega-projects also mean fewer regional employment centers; monitor whether PBA Holdings resubmits a smaller data center or pivots, and whether warehouse approval (2M SF already secured) proceeds as fallback, drawing warehouse workers and rental demand.

stories.title: Logistics Firm Highline Commerce Grows to 60K SF at Industry City, Thanks to Robots
stories.excerpt: Third-party logistics provider Highline Commerce is expanding its industrial footprint at Brooklyn’s Industry City for the second time in four years, Commercial Observer has learned. Highline’s lease renewal at 67 34th Street in Brooklyn’s Sunset Park neighborhood tacked on another 18,000 square feet to its warehouse space. The five-year deal rounds out the company’s total footprint at the mixed-use commercial campus to 60,000 square feet. The Brooklyn-based logistics company specializes in fulfilling orders for early-stage e-commerce brands, according to its website. Highline credits its growth, in part, to neighborly robots. The company has teamed up with fellow Industry City tenant Ultra Robotics to install “humanoid robots” that are fulfilling up to 30 percent of Highline’s clients’ orders, according to an Industry City spokesperson. The 60,000-square-foot flexible industrial space will remain largely dedicated to fulfillment operations, along with some office space and social rooms. Highline Commerce negotiated the deal directly with Industry City’s Jeff Fein and Brad Blum, who represent the landlord in-house. Asking rent was $28 per square foot. Highline arrived at Industry City from Manhattan’s Chelsea neighborhood in 2022. During its initial four-year lease, the company’s warehouse expanded from 27,000 square feet to 42,000 square feet. Highline plans to remain at Industry City as long as it can keep growing. “New York is in the middle of an e-commerce golden age, and brands are launching faster than the logistics world can support them,” Richard Hurley, founder and CEO of Highline, said in a statement. “Serving them doesn’t just take a village, it takes a place like Industry City, where on-site manufacturing, printing, fulfillment and the robots that run it all operate within feet of one another.” Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Highline Commerce, Industry City Brooklyn: expands to 60K SF (18K SF renewal); 5-year term
Fulfillment tenant; humanoid robots fulfill 30% of client orders via Ultra Robotics partnership
E-commerce logistics growth; automation-enabled warehouse density
stories.summary: Logistics provider Highline Commerce renewed and expanded its footprint at Industry City in Brooklyn's Sunset Park from 42K to 60K SF (18K SF add) on a five-year deal. The Brooklyn-based e-commerce fulfillment firm credits growth partly to deployment of humanoid robots via partnership with fellow Industry City tenant Ultra Robotics, which now fulfill up to 30% of Highline's clients' orders.
stories.impact: Highline's renewal and expansion reflects strong e-commerce order volumes post-pandemic and logistics innovation (humanoid automation). However, automation (robots fulfilling 30% of orders) may suppress labor demand per square foot, limiting warehouse-worker population inflows that would support rental housing. For TCR: Monitor whether Brooklyn's logistics hub (Industry City, neighboring warehouses) remains a significant employment driver for multifamily demand, or if automation erodes job growth despite volume increases. Highly automated warehouses may require fewer workers, reducing the tenant base for nearby workforce housing; track employment trends at major Brooklyn fulfillment centers to assess rental demand elasticity.

stories.title: Bakery Shop Cafe Aroma to Open New Spot on the Upper East Side
stories.excerpt: Time to rise and grind, Upper East Siders, as a new coffee shop and bakery is set to open at Avenues Equity Partners’ 1410 Madison Avenue in Carnegie Hill. Family-owned coffee shop and bakery Cafe Aroma has taken 2,200 square feet of retail space at the base of the five-story residential building, according to landlord broker Meridian Capital Group. The asking rent and the length of the lease were not disclosed. The average asking rent for retail space on the Upper East Side was $252 per square foot during the second quarter of 2026, according to data from CBRE. Meridian’s Jacob Mayer represented the landlord in this deal, while Shai Baradarian and Tamir Baradarian of eRealty Advisors represented Cafe Aroma. The Baradarians did not immediately respond to Commercial Observer’s request for comment. “1410 Madison Avenue is a rare turnkey restaurant space in a high-traffic Upper East Side location, and it generated strong interest from operators looking to be near Mount Sinai and Carnegie Hill’s dense residential base,” Mayer said in a statement. “Cafe Aroma is a great fit for the space, and we’re pleased to have completed a lease that positions both the landlord and the tenant for long-term success in the corridor.” This will be Cafe Aroma’s sixth Manhattan location and its first on the Upper East Side. Avenues Equity Partners acquired 1410 Madison Avenue and 1412 Madison Avenue for $6.3 million in January 2025, according to property records. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Cafe Aroma, UES Manhattan: 2.2K SF retail, 1410 Madison Ave (Carnegie Hill)
Avenues Equity Partners landlord; $252/SF Q2 2026 asking rent, upper East Side
Mount Sinai, dense residential nearby; turnkey restaurant space high demand
stories.summary: Family-owned Cafe Aroma leased 2,200 SF of retail space at Avenues Equity Partners' 1410 Madison Avenue, a five-story residential building in Carnegie Hill, Manhattan. The property is in a high-traffic Upper East Side location near Mount Sinai and dense residential base. Average UES retail asking rent was $252/SF in Q2 2026; lease terms not disclosed.
stories.impact: Strong UES retail demand near medical (Mount Sinai) and residential anchors signals healthy foot traffic and neighborhood occupier appetite; however, retail lease activity is a lagging indicator of residential health, not a demand driver for multifamily. For TCR: The fact that 1410 Madison Avenue is a mixed-use residential building with street-level retail suggests UES multifamily assets with ground-floor commercial are in demand. Monitor whether stabilized UES residential portfolios show consistent street-retail leasing (suggesting strong resident base/foot traffic) as a proxy for multifamily lease-up strength; retail occupancy can validate underlying residential unit demand.

stories.title: Musk’s companies prep $5M Seaholm office renovation
stories.excerpt: Downtown Austin’s Seaholm Power Plant, which was quietly occupied by several Elon Musk-affiliated companies earlier this year, is getting a $5.2 million facelift.  The planned renovations have been put in place by Wit Tech, a company with links to SpaceX, the firm that is pushing the construction of the Terafab semiconductor manufacturing plant in Grimes County. Musk’s companies occupy 35,000 square feet of space at 800 West Cesar Chavez Street, according to the Austin American Statesman. Musk is typically bombastic about his companies’ pursuits and ambitious, but has not yet publicly commented on the office space. He has given notes […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Seaholm Power Plant, Austin: Musk-affiliated firms occupy 35K SF; $5.2M renovation prep
Wit Tech (SpaceX-linked) driving renovations; 800 West Cesar Chavez, downtown
Grimes County Terafab semiconductor mega-project: $55B, 6K+ acres, 100M SF potential
stories.summary: Elon Musk-affiliated companies occupy 35,000 SF at Seaholm Power Plant (800 West Cesar Chavez) in downtown Austin and are preparing a $5.2 million renovation. Wit Tech, linked to SpaceX, is overseeing the work. This complements Musk's Terafab semiconductor fab in Grimes County, a $55 billion project on 6,000+ acres with potential to scale to 100 million SF. Grimes County Commissioners approved tax breaks and a tax increment reinvestment zone.
stories.impact: Musk's coordinated expansion—downtown Austin office hub + mega-fab in Grimes County—signals major employment-driven growth in Austin metro and exurban Bastrop/Grimes areas. Terafab's potential 100M SF footprint (if realized) would be transformational for Central Texas labor markets, attracting significant in-migration and wage pressure. For TCR: Monitor Terafab's construction ramp and hiring timeline; if fab scales to full capacity, Austin and Grimes County will experience material population inflows and rental demand acceleration across price points (tech/skilled workers + construction/operations labor). Downtown Austin office reactivation also signals retained urban employment. Identify multifamily investment windows in Austin MSA submarkets near tech corridors and potential future Grimes County workforce housing need.

stories.title: Florida’s largest Live Local Act project is bold. Its ambitious developer is controversial
stories.excerpt: Pablo Castro came into South Florida real estate with a bang. Over the past two years, the Spanish developer, who has never built in the U.S. and until recently had zero name recognition here, proposed Florida’s largest known multifamily complex, bought the site, secured approval and stirred controversy: Castro faces lawsuits by three local industry players who say they were stiffed after playing a role in laying the groundwork for the project.  HueHub would consist of 4,040 units across seven 34-story towers on 12 acres in West Little River. It is planned under the state’s Live Local Act, which allows […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: HueHub, West Little River Miami: 4,040 units, 7 towers, 34-story, 12 acres; Live Local Act
100% attainably priced, furnished; 40% affordable exceeds Live Local minimum; amenities free
Developer Pablo Castro (Spanish, unproven US track record) faces contractor/partner litigation
stories.summary: Spanish developer Pablo Castro is advancing HueHub, Florida's largest known multifamily project: 4,040 units across seven 34-story towers on 12 acres in West Little River under the state's Live Local Act. All units are furnished and attainably priced, exceeding the 40% affordable requirement. Free amenities (kitchens, fitness, learning center) differentiate the offering. However, Castro faces three lawsuits from local industry players alleging they were stiffed after helping lay groundwork for the project.
stories.impact: HueHub's scale (4,040 units) and aggressive affordability positioning (100% attainably priced) could significantly increase supply in Miami's affordable/workforce rental segment if delivered. However, Castro's litigation and lack of US track record raise execution risk and potential project delays or restructuring. For TCR: HueHub's approval and pre-construction momentum signal Miami's appetite for large, dense, affordable multifamily under Live Local Act incentives. Monitor whether other developers follow this model (large density + affordability incentives). If HueHub delivers on schedule, it will meaningfully increase affordable supply in West Little River and compress rents in that submarket; conversely, if project stalls due to litigation/financing, it removes a major supply headwind. Track Castro's contractor payment disputes and financing stability; project execution is uncertain.

stories.title: Faena Residences developers presell $30M penthouse for record price along Miami River
stories.excerpt: Shahab Karmely and Edgardo Defortuna’s joint venture presold a penthouse at their Faena-branded luxury condo development along the Miami River for $30 million, a record for the area on price as well as price per square foot.  Karmely’s KAR Properties has long planned to develop the waterfront site into the Rafael Viñoly-designed project. In 2022, Karmely brought on Defortuna’s Fortune International Group as a partner. Last year, they launched the Faena Residences.  A mystery buyer is under contract to buy a penthouse in the east tower that will span 8,200 square feet of interior space and also include 7,000 square […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Faena Residences, Miami River: penthouse presale $30M, 8.2K SF interior + 7K SF outdoor
Rafael Viñoly design; $3,700/SF pricing; record neighborhood benchmark
KAR Properties & Fortune International Group joint venture; presale ahead of groundbreak
stories.summary: Karmely's KAR Properties and Defortuna's Fortune International Group presold a penthouse at Faena Residences (Miami River) for $30 million, setting a neighborhood record. The duplex penthouse spans 8,200 SF interior plus 7,000 SF outdoor space (private rooftop, pool, wellness). At ~$3,700/SF, the presale occurs before construction and validates strong luxury buyer demand for presold units.
stories.impact: Faena Residences' $30M penthouse presale (and Mandarin Residences at $50M+ presales) reflect institutional confidence in Miami's ultra-luxury condo market and presale-to-construction financing models. However, luxury condo supply and presales do not directly compete with rental apartments. For TCR: Monitor if ultra-luxury condo presales (Miami River setting records) continue to attract capital and high-net-worth buyers, potentially drawing investor attention away from multifamily. Conversely, if condo presales cannibalize demand for high-end rentals, luxury multifamily rent growth may face headwinds in Miami. Track multifamily vs. condo ask prices in Miami River and related submarket to gauge competitive rental pressure.

stories.title: LA city’s top construction permits: Week ending July 11, 2026
stories.excerpt: 🏆 The largest development to receive permits last week was for a single-family build in Encino, where plans were filed for 11,278 square feet of work across two permits at 3864 North Kim Lane. The project includes a new two-story single-family dwelling with an attached three-car garage and a pond. Job Filing Numbers: 25010-10000-05697, 25010-10000-05874, 25047-10000-01751 and more 📊 In Valley Glen, permits were issued for 8,765 square feet of construction across three permits at 14414-14418 1/2 West Tiara Street. The project includes building a new accessory dwelling unit, along with a three-story duplex. The work is estimated to cost […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Top LA permits: Encino single-family, 11.3K SF; Valley Glen ADU+duplex 8.8K SF
Bel Air megamansion 'The One' alteration: 75.9K SF across 12 permits, $350K+
Single-family, ADU/duplex focus; residential detail permits, not multifamily pipeline
stories.summary: LA's top construction permits (week ending July 11, 2026) included a two-story single-family home in Encino (11,278 SF), an accessory dwelling unit plus three-story duplex in Valley Glen (8,765 SF, $880.5K cost), and a major alteration at Bel Air's 'The One' megamansion (75,856 SF across 12 permits, $350K+). Most significant permits were residential single-family and small multifamily (duplex) infill, not apartment community development.
stories.impact: LA's recent permit activity skews toward single-family, accessory dwelling units, and small multifamily infill rather than large-scale apartment development. This reflects zoning constraints (SB 9, SB 10 ADU provisions) and builder focus on small-lot, non-traditional multifamily products. For TCR: This signals weak near-term multifamily permitting in LA proper; developers are working around constraints via ADU and small-lot strategies rather than traditional apartment projects. Monitor city zoning reforms and density incentives to identify windows for larger multifamily entitled sites. ADU proliferation may add modest housing supply and suppress some rents, but is unlikely to materially compete with core multifamily demand.
